That the stock has been far more positively impacted than the sector as such is evident from the fact that its rise far exceeds that for the VanEck Rare Earth and Strategic Metals ETF (REMX), which has seen a robust 68% increase of its own.

As is usually the case with massive gains in small time periods, though, the stock's market valuations are now completely out of whack. For this reason, here I make a case for why the stock is a no-go for investors with the short- to medium-term time horizon in mind. But for long term investors, which, for the purpose of Long Term Tips is defined as at least five years, MP is still one to buy. There are risks, of course, which are highlighted as well.

Why has MP Materials exploded?

Before jumping into the valuations, first, let's look at why MP has exploded in the first place because this has implications for what can happen next. The stock prices trends in 2025 can be divided into two parts. The first is between January-July 10 and the second is July 10 onwards. The details are below:

Tariff challenges double MP in H1 2025

In H1 2025, the stock had already doubled. With the Trump administration's trade policy significantly increasing tariffs, rare earths came under challenge for two reasons. One, some 80% of US demand for them was met through imports and two, China, which was significantly impacted by these measures, was a substantial exporter of rare earths (see graphic below).

As China retaliated with requirements of export licenses, essentially making the process potentially more cumbersome at the very least, the supply of rare earths was at risk. This was a challenge since they have applications across industries, including the military.

Enter MP Materials, which produces over 10% of the world's rare earths and defines itself as America's "..only fully integrated rare earth producer with capabilities spanning the entire supply chain—from mining and processing to advanced metallization and magnet manufacturing". It's no wonder it suddenly looked more attractive.

DoD investment and Apple partnership

While China later eased the restrictions, the writing on the wall was clear. The US needed to secure supplies. The government took a big step in this direction with the help of the Defense Production Act, which was enabled by an executive order in March 2025. This allowed the government to enter into public-private partnerships.

The scene was now set for MP Materials as an investment target. By July 10, in what the company calls a "transformational" partnership, the DoD had bought preferred shares worth USD 400 million in the company, which translates into a 15% stake, making it the single biggest shareholder for the company. This investment, which will go towards building the magnet manufacturing 10x facility among other goals, led to over 50% jump in a single trading session on July 10.

USD 500 million Apple contract

That's hardly the end of the story, though. The DoD investment was followed by a USD 500 million long-term partnership with Apple (AAPL) to supply recycled rare earth magnets. Towards this end, along with the goals of the government investment, the company is now looking to expand its Fort Worth magnetics facility. The news led to another 20% jump in the stock in a single trading session

Q2 results reflect robust revenue growth and contracting losses

As the company released its Q2 2025 results on August 7, the stock received further support as the price rose by ~5% each in the two following trading days. It can be inferred here that the results were positive, what with the record production of NdPr and revenues surge by 84% YoY.

The company also managed to narrow its net loss by 24% YoY. While the loss is disappointing on the face of it, the fact that it's driven by its big capital investments in manufacturing offers comfort. These investments resulted in a 74% increase in operating expenses between 2022 and 2024 and also an operating loss.

Valuations are stretched for now...

With so much impetus for a stock price rise, MP's valuations have risen beyond any proximity of being reasonable. Consider this. Its TTM P/B ratio is at 11.1x compared to 2.33x for REMX at the end of August.

Next, as per consensus analyst estimates on Seeking Alpha, MP Materials will become profitable once again next year. But even then, its forward P/E is rather high 79.7x compared with REMX, which is at 40x right now. While REMX's forward ratio isn't immediately available, it can be safely assumed that for next year, it's likely to be even lower. This makes the potential gap between MP's and REMX's valuations even starker.

...but relax over the longer term

However, a few years down the line, MP's valuations are expected to cool down significantly. By 2029, that the stock's forward P/E cools down to below REMX's present levels, to 28.91x. And to even more palatable levels of 14.1x by 2030.

Since it's likely that REMX's levels would be even lower by then, I also compared MP with Energy Fuels (UUUU), the US based pure play uranium miner that acquired significant rare earth assets after purchasing Australia's Base Resources late last year. While its forward P/E for 2026 at 125.3x is even bigger than MP's, by 2029, it reduces to just 9.72x.

This raises the question - is there a Buy case for MP then? I'd say yes. Because even by 2029, its revenue will still be 1.4x that for Energy Fuels, maintaining its lead in rare earths production. And the lead would be even bigger than that suggested by the revenues, as Energy Fuels is also a uranium miner, which could well account for a significant part of its production, depending on the market conditions. Moreover, MP Materials has a history of rare earth production, while Energy Fuels has just got into it. And also MP has a history of profits, while UUUU is just getting started.

The risks

Despite my bullishness, however, there are risks to consider too. For one, by the time the long-term target period of 2030 is reached, another election would have taken place in the US. And that means a possible change in policy.

Even under the Trump administration, a de-escalation of the tariff war can't be ruled out, as evident from the fact that China has already relaxed on rare earth exports. If rare earth supply from China becomes abundant once again, domestic producers like MP Materials can lose their current edge.

Next, besides Energy Fuels, the heating up of rare earths could also result in more competition for MP Materials. Niron Magnetics being a case in point. The startup, with support from automobile majors like Stellantis (STLA) and General Motors (GM), is expected to become operational in 2027.

Investor takeaway

All things considered, there's still a Buy case for MP. But with all the risks involved, one that won't hurt the portfolio significantly. The global policy landscape is in a flux. And since that's the key driving force behind MP Materials' stock price right now, any change to it, can impact the stock going forward.

That said, there's no denying the significance of rare earths and the company's important position in supplying them in the US. Also, with US-China challenges being a long-standing reality of these times, the need for security of critical supplies can be expected to sustain. I'm going with a Buy rating, but only for long-term investors.
